mission.title: Install and Configure Connect Rpc on Kubernetes
mission.description: Connect is a slim library for building browser and gRPC-compatible HTTP APIs using Protocol Buffers. Installing Connect allows you to easily create type-safe clients and servers that support multiple protocols, making it ideal for modern web applications and microservices.

steps.title: Add the Connect RPC Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Connect RPC Helm repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add connectrpc https://connectrpc.com/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Connect RPC using Helm
steps.description:
Next, install the Connect RPC chart into your Kubernetes cluster. Replace `<release-name>` with your desired release name and `<namespace>` with the namespace you want to use:
```bash
helm install <release-name> connectrpc/connect --namespace <namespace> --create-namespace --version v1.19.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
After the installation, verify that the Connect RPC pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure your Connect RPC application runs efficiently, you can set resource limits. Edit the deployment with:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment <release-name> --namespace <namespace>
```
Add the following under `spec.containers.resources`:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up health checks
steps.description:
To enable health checks for your Connect RPC service, you can modify the deployment to include readiness and liveness probes. Edit the deployment again:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment <release-name> --namespace <namespace>
```
Add the following under `spec.containers`:
```yaml
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 30
  periodSeconds: 10
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the Connect RPC pods running in your specified namespace. You should also have resource limits and health checks configured for optimal performance.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add connectrpc https://connectrpc.com/charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Connect RPC installation, use the following command to uninstall the release. Replace `<release-name>` and `<namespace>` with your actual release name and namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall <release-name> --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the release, you may need to remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, if you no longer need the namespace, you can delete it. Ensure that all resources are cleaned up before doing so:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <namespace>
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up your current configuration and data. You can export the current release configuration with:
```bash
helm get values <release-name> --namespace <namespace> > backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and then upgrade the Connect RPC release to the latest version. Run the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade <release-name> connectrpc/connect --namespace <namespace> --version v1.20.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the Connect RPC pods are running with the new version. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the service is not reachable, ensure that the service is running and check the endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace <namespace>
kubectl describe svc <service-name> --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Deployment not scaling
troubleshooting.description:
If your deployment is not scaling as expected, check the deployment status and events:
```bash
kubectl get deployment <release-name> --namespace <namespace>
kubectl describe deployment <release-name> --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Health checks failing
troubleshooting.description:
If the liveness or readiness probes are failing, check the pod logs and probe configuration:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace> -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.containerStatuses[*].lastState}'
```

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
